issue_title: Multiple configs for the same backend type (e.g. 2 XMPP configs)
issue_body:
It'd make sense to allow multiple configurations for the same backend type in your config file. I propose doing this by adding a special `backend` parameter to options that is used instead of the name listed in the `backends` option and the backend's top level key. For example, to have a configs that send to different devices in pushover:

```
---
backends:
    - pushover
pushover:
    user_key: t0k3n
cell:
    user_key: t0k3n
    device: nexus5x
    backend: pushover
```

Then `ntfy send foobar` would send a notification to all your pushover devices, where as `ntfy -b cell send foobar` would only go to the device named `nexus5x` using pushover.

cc @danryder

issue_title: Need an efficient way to communicate between curio tasks and other threads
issue_body:
Following up on some discussion in #111:

A standard feature in event loops is some sort of `call_soon_threadsafe` call, which allows code running in another thread to schedule a callback onto the event loop. This is then used as a primitive to build various sorts of communication mechanisms between code running in the event loop and code running in the thread. The standard way of implementing this involves using a wake-up pipe as a safe cross-thread notification mechanism.

For curio, we also need to be able do this kind of communication, though it probably makes more sense to have some sort of queue instead of a `call_soon` API, because `call_soon` is a [task-spawning API, and curio tries to avoid task-spawning APIs](https://vorpus.org/blog/some-thoughts-on-asynchronous-api-design-in-a-post-asyncawait-world/#c-c-c-c-causality-breaker).

Right now, the way to do this is to use a regular `queue.Queue`, and on the curio side access it via `await run_in_thread(queue.Queue.get)` (or the equivalent sugar: `abide(queue.Queue.get)`). Under the hood, `run_in_thread` relies on the `future_wait` trap to detect when the `get` has finished, and `future_wait` uses a wake-up pipe mechanism.

So that's all fine, but it's inefficient: every task that wants to communicate with a worker thread has to commit a whole extra thread just to sitting around waiting for the `queue.Queue`'s internal `threading.Condition` variable to get signaled. If we use `run_in_thread`/`abide`, then these threads all live in the same fixed-size thread pool, so we might even starve out other users of `run_in_thread` or get deadlocks. (E.g. if our thread pool has N threads, and we spawn N workers, and each worker needs to send back a message, and each message needs a thread, then we need 2*N threads total, and if all the workers fill up the thread pool and then block waiting for messages to be sent and responded to, then the whole thing will deadlock.)

The solution is to provide some more direct exposure of the underlying wake-a-task-from-another-thread primitive, which could be used to build a `Queue` that can communicate between curio tasks and other threads without using the thread pool.

issue_title: Python 2: for class docstrings '__doc__' of 'type' objects is not writable
issue_body:
Snippet reproducing the problem:
```py
import docrep

docstrings = docrep.DocstringProcessor()


@docstrings.get_sectionsf('Base')
class Base(object):
    """ Base short description

    Base long description

    Parameters
    ----------
    param1 : int
    param2 : int
    """
    def __init__(self, param1, param2):
        pass


@docstrings.with_indent(4)
class Derived(Base):
    """ Derived short description.

    Derived long description.
    %(Base.parameters)s
    """
    pass
```

Full traceback:
```pytb
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AttributeError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
/tmp/test-docrep.py in <module>()
     20 
     21 @docstrings.with_indent(4)
---> 22 class Derived(Base):
     23     """ Derived short description.
     24 

/home/local/lesteve/miniconda3/envs/scratch27/lib/python2.7/site-packages/docrep/__init__.pyc in replace(func)
    374                 func = func.im_func
    375             func.__doc__ = func.__doc__ and self.with_indents(
--> 376                 func.__doc__, indent=indent, stacklevel=4)
    377             return func
    378         return replace

AttributeError: attribute '__doc__' of 'type' objects is not writable

```

We found a work-around of doing something like this instead:

```py

class Derived(Base):
   __doc__ = docstrings.with_indents(""" Derived class etc ... """)
```

Is that what the recommended approach?

One other minor thing catched my eye. I think following lines unnecessarily convert high UTF-8 codepoints to escapes:

    # escape all octets greater than 0x7F that are not part of a valid UTF-8
    output = ''.join(c if c <= '\x7f' else escape_bytes(to_raw(to_unicode(c, encoding))) for c in output)

RFC 4515 says that its needed only for bytes that make bytestring invalid UTF-8 otherwise (like mentioned in your comment) but allows regular multibyte characters unescaped (it is marked as UTFMB in ABNF grammar, UTFMB itself is specified in RFC 4512) but as `to_unicode()` is called on the `output` earlier, one can be pretty sure it contains valid characters only.

Float Comparison
issue_body:
https://github.com/pmorissette/bt/blob/d10205719711c46b7ccc798a5e2388486d3f24e7/bt/core.py#L1074

When comparing two floats that are meant to represent the same number, because of the limited precision of floats the comparison may return the wrong result.

In my case, 

    full_outlay = 1999.6937069886717
    amount = 1999.693706988672
    full_outlay > amount #True when we actually don't care about that level of precision

I am working on adding the condition
    np.isclose(full_outlay  - amount,0.)
to check for irrelevant precision.

issue_title: Return a pair from boltons.iterutils.bucketize
issue_body:
**EDIT**: nevermind, just read the docs for `partition`. Derp

Hey there,

Reading the docs of this lib and wondering why not return a pair from `bucketize`?

Current behavior:

    >>> bucketize(range(5))
    {False: [0], True: [1, 2, 3, 4]}
    >>> is_odd = lambda x: x % 2 == 1
    >>> bucketize(range(5), is_odd)
    {False: [0, 2, 4], True: [1, 3]}

Instead:

    >>> bucketize(range(5))
    {False: [0], True: [1, 2, 3, 4]}
    >>> is_odd = lambda x: x % 2 == 1
    >>> bucketize(range(5), is_odd)
    ([0, 2, 4], [1, 3])

I feel this will make client code more straightforward.

    >>> groups = bucektize(range(5), is_odd)
    >>> even_nums = groups[False]
    >>> odd_nums = groups[True]

vs.

    >>> even_nums, odd_nums = bucketize(range(5), is_odd)

Thoughts?

`diff -Naur GDriveFS-0.14.8-old/gdrivefs/gdtool/drive.py GDriveFS-0.14.8/gdrivefs/gdtool/drive.py`

`@@ -4,7 +4,7 @@`
` import random`
` import json`
` import time`
`-import httplib`
`+import http.client`
` import ssl `
` import tempfile`
` import pprint`
`@@ -54,7 +54,7 @@`
`         for n in range(0, 5): `
`             try:`
`                 return f(*args, **kwargs)`
`-            except (ssl.SSLError, httplib.BadStatusLine) as e:`
`+            except (ssl.SSLError, http.client.BadStatusLine) as e:`

issue_title: Please Update PLY Dependency to 3.9
issue_body:
I recently wrote a long bug report to the `sounddevice` team, who passed the buck to `CFFI`, whose developers implicated `pycparser` or `ply`. After lots of digging, I found a bug in PLY which prevented its use in a zipped-up source-less distributable as created by `py2app` or `pyinstaller`. After smugly filing a bug report with the PLY project, I checked out their upstream source so I could submit a PR only to find that... my bug had already been fixed!

It turns out that `pycparser` includes a *copy* of PLY 3.8 in your source tree, while PLY 3.9 is (currently) the latest release. 

Might I humbly request that you consider updating your packaged version of PLY to 3.9? I am sorry to say that `pycparser` is sufficiently over my head that I don't feel comfortable attempting a PR myself, so allow me to simply offer sincere THANKS!

See also:
https://github.com/spatialaudio/python-sounddevice/issues/68
https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/python-cffi/4JM22wGhVr4
https://github.com/dabeaz/ply/issues/108

issue_title: Consolidate string encoding handling across C API
issue_body:
UPDATE: final situation
=======================

*This issue has been fixed in PR #1052. Starting from version 5.3.0 psutil will fully support unicode. The notes below apply to *any* API returning a string such as process `exe()` or `cwd()` including non-filesystem related APIs such as process `username()` or `WindowsService.description()`. This is what users will get with psutil 5.3.0:*

* all strings are encoded by using the OS filesystem encoding which varies depending on the platform (e.g. UTF-8 on Linux, mbcs on Win)
* unicode is now correctly supported on Windows (no corrupted data is returned) by using specific unicode Windows APIs
* no API call is supposed to crash with ``UnicodeDecodeError``
* instead, in case of badly encoded data returned by the OS, the following error handlers are used to replace the bad characters in the string:
    * Python 3: `sys.getfilesystemencodeerrors()` (PY 3.6+) or ``"surrogatescape"`` on POSIX and ``"replace"`` on Windows
    * Python 2: ``"replace"``
* on Python 2 all APIs return bytes (``str`` type), never ``unicode``
* on Python 2 you can go back to unicode by doing: 
  `unicode(p.exe(), sys.getdefaultencoding(), errors="replace")`and do funky string comparisons 

Original issue
==========

*(NOTE: this content is updated as I go)*

So, psutil has different APIs returning a string, many of which misbehaving when it comes to unicode.

* A: may raise decoding error on python 3 in case of non-ASCII string
* B: return `unicode` on Python 2 instead of `str`
* C returns incorrect / invalid encoded data in case of non-ASCII string

| API                         | Linux | Win         | OSX | FreeBSD | NetBSD | OpenBSD | SunOS |
|-----------------------------|-------|-------------|-----|---------|--------|---------|-------|
| `Process.cmdline()`         |       |             |     |         |        |         |       |
| `Process.connections()`     |       |             | A   | A       | A      |         |       |
| `Process.cwd()`             |       |             |     |         |        |         |       |
| `Process.environ()`         |       | B           |     |         |        |         |       |
| `Process.exe()`             |       |             |     |         |        |         |       |
| `Process.memory_maps()`     |       | B,C         | A   | A       |        |         | A     |
| `Process.name()`            |       |             |     |         |        |         |       |
| `Process.open_files()`      |       |             |     | A       |        |         |       |
| `Process.username()`        |       |             |     |         |        |         |       |
|                             |       |             |     |         |        |         |       | 
| `disk_io_counters()`        |       |             |     |         |        |         |       |
| `disk_partitions()`         | A     |             | A   | A       | A      | A       | A     |
| `disk_usage(str)`           |       |             |     |         |        |         |       |
| `net_connections()`         |       |             |     | A       | A      |         |       |
| `net_if_addrs()`            |       |             |     |         |        |         |       |
| `net_if_stats()`            |       | B           |     |         |        |         |       |
| `net_io_counters()`         |       |             |     |         |        |         |       |
| `sensors_fans()`            |       |             |     |         |        |         |       |
| `sensors_temperatures()`    |       |             |     |         |        |         |       |
| `users()`                   | A     |             | A   | A       | A      | A       | A     |
|                             |       |             |     |         |        |         |       | 
| `WinService.binpath()`      |       | B           |     |         |        |         |       |
| `WinService.description()`  |       | B,C         |     |         |        |         |       |
| `WinService.display_name()` |       | B,C         |     |         |        |         |       |
| `WinService.name()`         |       |             |     |         |        |         |       |
| `WinService.status()`       |       |             |     |         |        |         |       |
| `WinService.username()`     |       | B           |     |         |        |         |       |

Right now there are 3 distinctive problems about it.

Filesystem or locale encoding?
==============================

First problem is that the C extension currently uses 2 approaches when it comes to decode and return a string:

* `PyUnicode_DecodeFSDefault`
    * `PyUnicode_Decode(Py_FileSystemDefaultEncoding, "replace")` on Python 2 (kinda equivalent)
* `PyUnicode_DecodeLocale` (Python 3 only)

Most of the times we use `PyUnicode_DecodeFSDefault` but not always. First issue, then, is to figure out which APIs should use one or the other. It appears clear that `PyUnicode_DecodeFSDefault` should be used for all fs-related APIs such as process `exe()`, `open_files()` etc. It is less clear when to use `PyUnicode_DecodeLocale`. To my understanding **maybe** we should use it for things such as:

* `WindowsService.description()`
* `WindowsService.display_name()`

...and **maybe** (but less likely) for:

* `Process.username()`
* `users()`

*UPDATE: decided it's better for the user to deal with one encoding only (filesystem) and not think about what API he/she is using*

Error handling
==============

Second question is what to do in case the string cannot be correctly decoded.

**About FS APIs**

Right now we tend to use `"surrogateescape"`, which is also the default for `PyUnicode_DecodeFSDefault` on Python 3, so I'm pretty sure for fs-related paths we should do this every time we have the chance (on Python 3 at least).

Note: on Windows the default is `"surrogatepass"` (py 3.6) or `"replace"` as per [PEP-529](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0529/).

It must be noted that AFAIK on Python 2 the `os` module has no fs-APIs returning a string (e.g. `os.listdir()`) which may crash with `UnicodeDecodeError` so we should do the same and use `"replace"`. There are already some tests for this, see see [test_unicode.py](https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/blob/master/psutil/tests/test_unicode.py)).

**About other APIs**

Shall we use `"strict"` (and raise exception) or `"surrogateescape"`? Not sure.

Python 2 vs. 3
===========

And here comes the troubles. Whereas it appears kind of clear what to do on Python 3, Python 2 is different. In order to attempt to correctly handle and represent all kind of strings on Python 2 we should return... well, `unicode` instead of `str`, but I don't want to do that, and neither have APIs which return two different types depending on the circumstance. Since unicode support is already broken in Python 2 and its stdlib (see [bpo-18695](http://bugs.python.org/issue18695)) I'm happy to always return `str`, use `"replace"` error handler and consider unicode support in psutil + python 2 broken (UPDATE: it turns out it's not).

There's still the question about when to use `PyUnicode_DecodeFSDefault` and (a variant of) `PyUnicode_DecodeLocale` but on Python 2 this is less important as unicode handling is broken anyway.

Summary / TODO
==============

**Python 3**

- figure out whether / when to use `PyUnicode_DecodeFSDefault` and `PyUnicode_DecodeLocale`
- figure out the default error handler for `PyUnicode_DecodeLocale` (if used)
- discover APIs which do not use `PyUnicode_DecodeFSDefault` and as such may crash

**Python 2**

- same as above but never fail with `UnicodeDecodeError` in case of `PyUnicode_DecodeLocale`
- never return `unicode`, always `str` and have tests for it

issue_title: non unicode data not correctly handled in Python 3
issue_body: Filenames and command line arguments can include (non-unicode) binary data on most operating systems.  This is more difficult to handle on Python 3 because these are generally expressed as the str type.  I've created pull request https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/pull/733 with a test case that highlights the issue but do not have a solution at this point.

                permissions = True
            else:
                logger.error(f"Can't send the message : don't know where to send it")

        if not permissions:
            logger.warning("No permissions to speak here")
            return False

        # Where can be a TextChannel or a DMChannel
        # from_ is a Member or None
        # ctx can be a Context or None

        try:
            await where.send(message, embed=embed)
        except Exception as e:
            if try_ >= 3:
                logger.warning(f"Could not send {message} to channel after 3 tries")
                logger.exception("Exception for not sending is :")
                return False
            else:
                await self.send_message(ctx=ctx, from_=from_, where=where, message=original_message, embed=embed, can_pm=can_pm, force_pm=force_pm, mention=mention, try_=try_+1)

issue_title: Replace req, resp, meta, params, kwargs, etc. with more general context parameter that can store all of this information
issue_body:
This is an enchancement that will surely break compatibility but it will be a solution to problem with deciding which method should have access to which of this basic data structures.

Because there is always single resource instance on given route no state can be stored in this object between method calls. This means that all required data must be always passed to each method that requires it. Because of that `graceful` needs to make very opinionated decision about what is available on each step of processing the request. Although this helps developer to focus on what is important in specific method handler (e.g., retrieve, list, create in generic views) it is very hard to extend if something non usual needs to be done - often it requires overriding of many methods.

We could also consider if this context could be passed to serialisers so more complex things could be done like: field serialisation that depends on query string parameters.

issue_title: SubscribeCOVPropertyRequest sequence Elements have incorrect optional flag
issue_body:
BACnet standard (EN ISO 16484-5) states that request SubscribeCOVPropertyRequest sent without issueConfirmedNotifications and/or lifetime and/or covIncrement is a valid request (e.g. without issueConfirmedNotifications  and lifetime it means subscription cancellation).
I think it would be wise to change this request class to:

`class SubscribeCOVPropertyRequest(ConfirmedRequestSequence):`
`    serviceChoice = 28`
`sequenceElements = \`
        `[ Element('subscriberProcessIdentifier', Unsigned, 0)`
     `   , Element('monitoredObjectIdentifier', ObjectIdentifier, 1)`
  `      , Element('issueConfirmedNotifications', Boolean, 2, True)`
   `     , Element('lifetime', Unsigned, 3, True)`
`        , Element('monitoredPropertyIdentifier', PropertyReference, 4)`
 `       , Element('covIncrement', Real, 5, True)`
 `       ]`

to be compliant with the standard.
Of course I can provide the fix if I'm accepted as contributor.

issue_title: Allow characteristic properties like minValue to be overridden.
issue_body:
It should be possible to configure the properties of a characteristic to align with the functionality of the device and the iOS UI. For example, say your window covering can go up only to 50% open and that it is either closed or open. HAP specifies the default values for the coverings target position as maxValue=100 and minStep=0.1. This means iOS clients can slide at 0.1 step all the way to 100%, which you must map to all-up or all-down in your code.

Instead, you can do:
```python
service = loader.get_serv_loader().get("WindowCovering")
pos = service.get_characteristic("TargetPosition")
pos.properties["maxValue"] = 50
pos.properties["minStep"] = 50
pos.hap_template = pos._create_hap_template()
```

Now iOS correctly allows you to only fully go up or down and the max display value is 50%. The hacky thing about the above is the last line and this is the topic of this issue - overriding properties should be more "native".

issue_title: Observing the close & other forms of lookahead bias
issue_body:
Hi,

I have read this article:
http://blog.quantopian.com/9-mistakes-quants-make-that-cause-backtests-to-lie-by-tucker-balch-ph-d/

I would like to know your opinion about the point 3 and how backtrader could cover it.

3. Observing the close & other forms of lookahead bias

In this failure mode, the quant assumes he can observe market closing prices in order to compute an indicator, and then also trade at the close. As an example, one might use closing price/volume to calculate a technical factor used in the strategy, then trade based on that information.

This is a specific example of lookahead bias in which the strategy is allowed to peek a little bit into the future. In my work I have seen time and again that even a slight lookahead bias can provide fantastic (and false) returns.

Other examples of lookahead bias have to do with incorrect registration of data such as earnings reports or news. Assuming for instance that one can trade on the same day earnings are announced even though earnings are usually announced after the close.

Solution: Don’t trade until the open of the next day after information becomes available.

issue_title: Derive developmental version build number from SCM through git describe
issue_body:
Originally discussed in #77.

It seems like bumpversion relies on information coded in files (`.bumpversion.cfg`, `setup.py`) and cannot read information provided by SCM.

When building PEP440 *developmental releases* (maybe in CI), the build number (how many commits since the last release) should be obtained from SCM (`git describe`) and and appended to version (e.g. `1.2.3.dev0`, `1.2.3.dev1`, ...).

Something to take a look at is https://pypi.python.org/pypi/setuptools-version-command, which already does this (in a bit different way).

issue_title: Do not crash on too long sheet name
issue_body:
Hi,

The sheet name has a hard limit of 31 characters due to some Excel arbitrary limit. XlsxWriter raises an error if a user tries to create a sheet with a longer name.

This is an issue if the sheetname is provided by the user and forces to developer to verify the name size.

Here is some code that demonstrates the problem:
```python

import xlsxwriter

sheetname = input("Please specify a sheet name")  # user provided name**
workbook = xlsxwriter.Workbook('hello.xlsx')
worksheet = workbook.add_worksheet(sheetname)  # crash if longer than 31
workbook.close()
```

While fixing it in the above code is easy, it forces the developer to implement a check for a requirement I consider as low level and that could easily be implemented in the library itself.
Let say that Excel 2017 increases the limit to 50, it will be easy to change this limit in an upgrade of XlsxWriter instead of forcing each developer using the library to modify their code (with the condition that they are aware of this low level change).

Automatically reducing the sheetname is not likely to bother the end users while stopping them with an error is always unpleasant.

We have this issue at odoo/odoo#12788 where the sheet name is based on a translation where we don't have an easy control on the content.

Thanks

issue_title: Retrying the same URL
issue_body:
I realized that quite often I encounter such a pattern:

* Take a `betamax_session` pytest fixture, inject it into a class I'm testing
* Using this class create some *task* making a POST-request
* Make GET requests to a URL until some conditions are met (ex. task is ready)

Sure enough betamax caches the first GET response which tells that the abstract task is still in progress, therefore my tests fail.

Can I solve this problem configuring a betamax somehow? Current project I am working on returns a 206 status code in case task isn't ready yet, so in theory I could create my own matcher which does not serialize such requests but as far as I understand I can't do that per-test with the betamax_session fixture? 

Do have any other ideas on how to address the problem? Right now I use a `time.sleep(1)` crutch.

I found a problem related to ISCP command OSDMENU and other OSDxxxx subcommands. Here are the details:

onkyo OSDMENU

<eISCP(unknown) 192.168.36.144:60128>: OSDMENU

issue_title: Handle encoding for numpy array scalars
issue_body:
The `json_tricks` library will serialize instances of `numpy.ndarray` but if you pass it an array scalar (e.g., a single element of an array, which have datatypes like `numpy.int64`, `numpy.float64`, etc.)

For example:

```python
>>> z = np.array([1,2,3])  
>>> json_tricks.dumps(z)
'{"dtype": "int64", "shape": [3], "__ndarray__": [1, 2, 3]}'
>>> json_tricks.dumps(z[0])
TypeError: Object of type <class 'numpy.int64'> could not be encoded by TricksEncoder using encoders [<function json_date_time_encode at 0x7f38028fea60>, <function class_instance_encode at 0x7f38028febf8>, <function json_complex_encode at 0x7f38028fec80>, <function numpy_encode at 0x7f3802410400>]
```

My present workaround is to ensure that all array scalars are converted to numpy arrays before attempting to serialize (`json_tricks.dumps(np.array(z[0]))` in the above) but it would be easier if this was handled automatically.

issue_title: Move action definition to prod/rule meta-data
issue_body:
Current syntax for defining actions is:

```
@action_name
Rulename: ...;
```

If action definition would be moved to `{}` block together with disambiguation and other meta-data (issue #57)  a fine-grained control would be possible. As `{}` block is defined by production, each production could have a different action. This would make current list-based specification of actions deprecated.

After #17 is implemented additional flexibility is achieved. Action could be given per rule as it is now, and overriden per production.

Syntax might be similar to what it's now but the `@...` would be given inside `{}` block.

```
Rulename: .... {@action_name};
```
Or for each production defined in one rule (after #17 is implemented):
```
Rulename {@action_name}: ...;
```

issue_title: Do RetryExecutor and TimeoutExecutor work well together?
issue_body:
In the TimeoutExecutor implementation from 1.7.0, futures are cancelled on timeout.  Does that work properly with the RetryExecutor?

My expectation:

- If the *outer* future is cancelled, that should be passed through and RetryExecutor stops handling the future -> that probably works now, so a TimeoutExecutor wrapping a RetryExecutor probably behaves sensibly.
- If the *inner* future is cancelled, what does RetryExecutor do; does it retry?  I think the policy should be able to decide.  It should be a possibility to retry when the inner future is cancelled, so that a RetryExecutor wrapping a TimeoutExecutor can work (can retry on timeout).

issue_title: Smart Iteration with reverse()'d queryset has a broken report
issue_body:
When using a queryset with `.reverse()` applied and `report_progress=True`, you get a report that ends:

`highest pk so far 1234`

The number at the end keeps falling, and the re-writing process (writing `\r` then rewriting the string) then screws up when you drop one decimal place, for example when you hit the pk 999 the report actually reads:

`highest pk so far 9994`

Because that `4` is not erased. If we could write out as many spaces as required to the end of the line, *then* `\r`, it should always work to erase the text.

issue_title: Readonly revision_view for DeserializationError and IntegrityError
issue_body:
If I make a migration changing a field from a `CharField` to a `ForeignKey` and then go to look at a previous version where that field was saved as a character string, I get a `DeserializationError` error which means that I can't even see the representation of the object from that revision.

To overcome this I've created a kind of read only view that just displays the `serialized_data` in a simple JSON tree if a `DeserializationError` or `IntegrityError` is raised in the `revision_view`. A user can't revert to this revision but at least they can see what the data was at that revision.

I'm not sure if this something that would be useful to the project or if it's the right approach (or if there is something already built in that I'm missing). I'm using [this branch](https://github.com/etianen/django-reversion/compare/master...kevinmarsh:revision_readonly) for a project at work but it'd need more work/tests/thought before it got integrated into `master`.


For example before:
![deserializationerror_before](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1423728/6893709/ef091e1a-d6c6-11e4-8a82-0e34d91f49fa.png)


and after:

issue_title: open redirect vulnerability
issue_body:
We discovered recently while experimenting that a site using django-saml2-auth to authenticate against Okta is vulnerable to an [open redirect vulnerability](https://blog.qualys.com/securitylabs/2016/01/07/open-redirection-a-simple-vulnerability-threatens-your-web-applications). For example, a maliciously crafted URL like `https://safe.site/accounts/login_okta?next=//evil.site` will redirect the user to Okta for authentication, which will then redirect them to `evil.site` after they successfully authenticate. The evil site operator can then engage in a phishing attack, or even take advantage of the user's `safe.site` credentials to compromise the original site.

PR #17 was filed to address this issue.

issue_title: Properly handle requests that do multiple writes (e.g. by aiohttp 3)
issue_body:
The aiohttp client in version 3 does (at least) two writes if the request has a body: one for the headers, a second for the body. This was done in aio-libs/aiohttp#2126 and is documented in the "Are there changes in behavior for the user?" section of the description. Check out the [diff of the write_headers function](https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/commit/bbf62184929f8f4ae36ad417005b5f9dad4d9e6a#diff-b01a901427ef6052963c0c1281a51872L241).

With mocket, this is not handled correctly. The body is lost. Would it be possible to implement proper handling of this scenario?

Example test failures: https://travis-ci.org/sjaensch/aiobravado/jobs/343058521

[Test 1 source](https://github.com/sjaensch/aiobravado/blob/master/tests/functional/request_func_test.py#L15)
[Test 2 source](https://github.com/sjaensch/aiobravado/blob/master/tests/functional/model_func_test.py#L177)

issue_title: Use LDAP bind instead of check_password
issue_body:
A common pattern for LDAP authentication is to bind against the LDAP server using the target user's DN and password, which means you'd never need to see/use/compare the userPassword attribute at all.  As such, my LDAP ACLs make it such that userPassword is never returned by LDAP, in any form.

The CAS LDAP implementation uses check_password(), which requires the LDAP server to return userPassword so that it can be compared with the POSTed value. I would prefer to bind against the server with the user's credentials, instead.

issue_title: Read the docs
Swagger doc: superfluous escaping for path parameters
issue_body:
To reproduce:

1) Run the following minimal example
```
from flask import Flask
from flask_restplus import Api, Resource

app = Flask(__name__)
api = Api(app, doc = '/doc')

@api.route('/<path:path>')
class Path(Resource):
    def get(self, path):
        return path

if __name__ == '__main__':
    app.run()
```

2) Navigate to Swagger documentation at http://localhost:5000/doc#!/default/get_path

3) Enter path parameter value `"just/a/test"`. The generated request URL is http://localhost:5000/just%2Fa%2Ftest , but it is expected that it is http://localhost:5000/just/a/test .
Both URLs do actually work, but the unescaped one is clearly preferable, for aesthetic reasons.

issue_title: MailChimpClient resuse connection pool and keep-alive
issue_body:
`MailChimpClient` open a new connection for every request. It's faster to reuse the connection pool and keep-alive. So, the `MailChimpClient` should open and keep the requests session.
Also, if the `MailChimpClient` would implement the session, then it should have some `close()` and/or `__exit__()` methods to close the session manually.

issue_title: Please make string escaping optional (i.e. clean_metric_name() should be optional)
issue_body:
Thank you for the good work on `graphitesend`!

I have a [small project](https://github.com/mpenning/graphite-gardener) which sends stats to graphite via `send_list(stats_list)`, where `stats_list` is a list of tuples.  The problem is that I'm trying to replace an existing [collectd Write_Graphite plugin](https://collectd.org/wiki/index.php/Plugin:Write_Graphite), and that plugin escapes stats slightly differently than `graphitesend` does.

Of course, I could always just open a tcp socket and dump into graphite, but I'm hoping to use `graphitesend` instead.

I'd like a configurable `auto_escape` option, like this...

```
    gs = graphitesend.init(graphite_server=self.server,
        graphite_port=self.port,
        prefix=self.prefix,
        system_name=self.host.name,
        group='snmp',
        auto_escape=False)
      #^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
```

The default for `auto_escape` should be True for backwards compatibility. If `auto_escape` is False, `clean_metric_name()` should never be called on metrics.  That leaves me responsible for escaping characters, which is just fine by me.

I can submit a PR if you like.

issue_title: The Jinja2 'Include' statement is not supported by docxtpl
issue_body:
Hello!

I'm trying to use the [Jinja2 `Include` statement](http://jinja.pocoo.org/docs/templates/#include) in my template. However, it does not appear to be supported by docxtpl.

Here's what I've tried and the issues I've run into:

I have a simple `test.docx` file with the following contents:

```
{% include 'sub.docx' %}
```

In the same folder, I have the file `sub.docx` with the following contents:

```
A sub-document
```

# No loader specified

I try the following code:

```python
from docxtpl import DocxTemplate

doc = DocxTemplate('test.docx')
context = {}
doc.render(context)
```

This gives me the error:

```
TypeError: no loader for this environment specified
```

# Unicode Decode Error

I noticed that the `render` function takes an optional `jinja_env` parameter. So I tried to specify the `Environment` manually:

```python
from jinja2 import Environment, FileSystemLoader
env = Environment(loader=FileSystemLoader("/path/to/template/folder"))
doc.render(context, env)
```

But then I get this error message:

```
UnicodeDecodeError: 'utf-8' codec can't decode byte 0xd2 in position 16: invalid continuation byte
```

# Template Syntax Error

Since that didn't work, I figured out from the Jinja2 documentation that you can specify the template encoding when creating the loader. So I tried:

```python
env = Environment(loader=FileSystemLoader("/path/to/template/folder", encoding='latin-1'))
doc.render(context, env)
```

Then I get this error message:

```
jinja2.exceptions.TemplateSyntaxError: unexpected char '\x05' at 8902
```

I'm out of ideas. Any suggestions on how to make this work? Thanks!

issue_title: Missing referer header in flash fallback
issue_body:
The flash fallback doesn't send the HTTP Referer header.

The reason I need it, if your curious, is due to [Django's CSRF protection](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/ref/csrf/#how-it-works). When requesting via SSL, it confirms that the referer matches the sites domain. Without the referer header, every request will 403.

issue_title: Support for custom routers
issue_body:
Currently, `rest_framework_swagger.urlparser.UrlParser` uses a hardcoded value to determine if an endpoint is a router.

This is done by comparing the view's` __module__` to `rest_framework.routers`.

This will return false if the user has replaced the router with a subclass.


Simply checking the `__name__` against `APIRoot` works, but feels less than ideal. Depending on how acceptable the overhead is, it may be worth doing a check on the `__mro__` to see if `rest_framework.routers.DefaultRouter.get_api_root_view().APIRoot` is in there, but as it's dynamically generated, that will still involve string comparisons.

I packaged `python-gevent-eventemitter` for Arch GNU/Linux in AUR:

Packages: [python2-gevent-eventemitter-git](https://aur.archlinux.org/packages/python2-gevent-eventemitter-git/), [python-gevent-eventemitter-git](https://aur.archlinux.org/packages/python-gevent-eventemitter-git/)

Packages prefixed with `python-` are built for Python 3.x, and prefixed with `python2-` are built for Python 2.x

You can install them using any AUR helper (the recommended one is `pacaur`):
```
pacaur -S python-gevent-eventemitter-git
```

You can also install them manually:
```
git clone https://aur.archlinux.org/python-gevent-eventemitter-git.git
cd python-gevent-eventemitter-git
makepkg -sic
```

It would be great if you added them to your readme

```python
import mxnet as mx

def _array(shape):
    """Create an NDArray with random entries and the given shape
    """
    return mx.nd.random.uniform(-1.0, 1.0, shape=shape)

def index(sc, i, j):
    """Equivalent to sc[i, j] in numpy
    """
    return sc.take(i).squeeze(axis=0)  \
             .take(j).squeeze(axis=0)

# tweaking `sc` in different shapes, the code sometimes crashes, sometimes not
row_len, col_len = 2, 8 

# the scanned variable
sc = _array((row_len, col_len))
sc.attach_grad()  # we only require the gradient w.r.t. `sc`

# `i`, `j` are loop variables which don't require grad
i = mx.nd.array([0])
j = mx.nd.array([0])

with mx.autograd.record(train_mode=True):
    xs = []
    for _i in range(row_len):
        x_i = []
        for _j in range(col_len):
            x_ij = index(sc, i, j)
            x_i.append(x_ij)
            j = j + 1
        i = i + 1
        j = j - col_len  # reset j
        xs.append(mx.nd.stack(*x_i))
    x = mx.nd.stack(*xs)
    x = x.sum()

x.backward()
print(sc.grad.asnumpy())   # the expected result should be all `1`s
```

## Steps to reproduce
(Paste the commands you ran that produced the error.)

1. Save the code as `test.py`, and run `python2 test.py`

## What have you tried to solve it?

I have no idea where it comes from. That by tweaking `row_len` and `col_len`, it behaves differently seems weird to me. I only observed that
1. When `row_len = 1`, it never crashes
2. When `row_len = 2`, and `col_len < 8`, it doesn't crash; when `col_len >= 8`, it crashes
3. When `row_len = 3`, and `col_len < 7`, it doesn't crash; when `col_len >= 7`, it crashes
4. When `row_len = 4`, and `col_len < 7`, it doesn't crash; when `col_len >= 7`, it crashes

For bugs or installation issues, please provide the following information.
The more information you provide, the more likely people will be able to help you.

R `sessionInfo()`:

## Error Message:
no error message

## Steps to reproduce
I was training resnet50 using train_imagenet.py in mxnet/example/image-classification folder. I compiled mxnet with CUDA 9, CUDNN 7.0 and run it on four V100 GPUs.

I tried nvidia branch of Caffe and got close to 2x speedup with FP16 compared to FP32. 

Regards,
Rengan

issue_title: No module named bbox when running rcnn demo.py
issue_body:
I have met probelm when running the demo.py in rcnn.
The exception says :

"File "/mnt/gelu/mxnet/example/rcnn/rcnn/processing/bbox_transform.py", line 2, in 
from ..cython.bbox import bbox_overlaps_cython
ImportError: No module named bbox"

I'm pretty sure that i have compiled and installed all operators.

I have solve the problem by doing the following steps:

I make the following changes to the project and fix the problem:
1.add following lines of code before import '..cython.bbox import bbox_overlaps_cython' in rcnn/processing/bbox_transform.py:
	import pyximport
	pyximport.install()

2.edit the setup.py in rcnn/cython/:
  change the code from ['nms_kernel.cu', 'gpu_nms.pyx'], to ['nms_kernel.cu', 'gpu_nms.cpp'],

3. run the command: python setup.py install under the above directory

4. copy 3 .so files from rcnn/cython/build/lib.linux-x86_64-2.7/ to ~/.pyxbld/lib.linux-x86_64-2.7/rcnn/cython


I just want to ask is there any more nice way to solve this problem ?

issue_title: Report span.timestamp and duration
issue_body:
While possible to derive a span's timestamp and duration at query time, it is best to report them to zipkin. This reduces the guesswork on the server side, and particularly makes the cassandra storage implementation more effective. This also makes the duration query operable.

### Do we need to do anything?
Here's the normal doc on that zipkin.io/pages/instrumenting.html This elaborates timestamp and duration a bit, particularly that they can be supplied independently of annotations (sometimes more accurately that way, as a stopwatch isn't at risk of NTP updates)

Then, we'd need to ensure that the special-case of [continuing a client-originated span on the server](https://github.com/openzipkin/openzipkin.github.io/issues/49) is addressed (where B3 headers are read). This might happen here or in pyramid, depending on if we introduce B3 extraction here or not.

Here are tests that should be in place to ensure that span.timestamp and duration are set properly.

* client creates a span and propagates it to a server
  * client propagates b3 headers for trace id, span id, and sampled=1
  * server neither reports span.timestamp nor duration
  * client reports span.timestamp and duration

* server creates a new trace
  * no b3 headers are detected
  * server reports span.timestamp and duration

* server creates a new trace with externally supplied ids
  * caller propagates b3 headers for trace id, span id, but *no sampled header*
  * server reports span.timestamp and duration

Does this make sense? Can anyone here help implement this?

See https://github.com/openzipkin/brave/issues/277#issuecomment-262429392

issue_title: REQ to ROUTER-DEALER to REP multiprocessing issue
issue_body:
I'm using the REQ - REP pattern with a router-dealer in between to handle multiple requests to a single REP service. It seems that the router/dealer pattern is not working with multiprocessing.

Threading works:
```
thread = threading.Thread(target=worker_thread, args=(worker_uri,))
```

multiprocessing does not:
```
thread = multiprocessing.Process(target=worker_thread, args=(worker_uri,))
```

```
worker_uri = "tcp://127.0.0.1:10103"
```

Typical router/dealer pattern:
```
workers = context.socket(zmq.ROUTER)
workers.bind(...worker uri ...)
socket = context.socket(zmq.DEALER)
socket.bind (... socket uri ...)
zmq.device(zmq.QUEUE, socket, workers)
```

Until I can figure this out, I have to use threads with the router/dealer then python multiprocessing pipes for the handoff to the processes.

issue_title: Tracking data lineage
issue_body:
<!---
Currently, there are no strict procedures or guidelines for submitting issues.
In short, please just use common sense.

Common sense includes this at bare-minimum:

 * search for similar issues posted before creating a new issue.

Also, add steps to reproduce the bug, if applicable. Sample code would be nice too :)

For more information on how to submit valuable contributions,
see https://opensource.guide/how-to-contribute/#how-to-submit-a-contribution
-->

My application requires a stringent record of lineage for the data it processes. Is there a standard way for a Luigi pipeline to yield this kind of self-reporting information?

My DAGs are dynamically generated, so I need a product that comes out of the pipeline to be accompanied by a record of the processing chain it underwent, with enough detail on parameters that the graph could be reconstructed based on this.

The best things I've come up with so far would be passing along a special input/output through the Tasks that would contain the graph a dataset has passed through up to that point, or instead traversing back through the graph on its completion to build this record as a final processing step. I don't know the system well enough to know which of these would be more idiomatic, so if anyone has prior experience solving this problem that would be really helpful.

issue_title: In Sample Program - Instantiation of Browser class should also include the browser name parameter
issue_body:
***What is the Issue ?***

In https://splinter.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tutorial.html. When creating an browser instance we need to pass it a parameter, i.e the name of the browser. But the ReadMe doesn't state anything about it. We have this issue when the issue when the use has more than one browser installed.

***The Error that we see if no parameter is passed during Browser instantiation is:***

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "./sample.py", line 6, in <module>
    browser = Browser()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/splinter/browser.py", line 63, in Browser
    return driver(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/splinter/driver/webdriver/firefox.py", line 39, in __init__
    self.driver = Firefox(firefox_profile)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/selenium/webdriver/firefox/webdriver.py", line 81, in __init__
    self.binary, timeout)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/selenium/webdriver/firefox/extension_connection.py", line 51, in __init__
    self.binary.launch_browser(self.profile, timeout=timeout)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/selenium/webdriver/firefox/firefox_binary.py", line 67, in launch_browser
    ......
```
***How to resolve this:***

It would be better to clearly state in the line no. 3 of the sample program ( https://splinter.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tutorial.html ) that the user might need to pass the name of the browser as a parameter, something like this:

`browser = Browser('chrome')`  or 
`browser = Browser() # you might need to pass parameter(i.e your browser name) during Browser instance creation Browser('name of browser') `

I am reporting this because I recently started using the Splinter API and I found it hard to figure out why the sample program code was not running. 

Hope this helps someone else as well 😄 
Thank you!

issue_title: Make it an error to unregister a locator strategy that hasn't been registered
issue_body: Currently if you try to unregister a locator strategy that doesn't exist, there's only a notification in the log file that no such strategy exists and nothing is done. This can lead to problems if, for example, there's a typo in the strategy name and the actual strategy is accidentally not unregistered. Better make this situation an error so that users see if there has been a problem.

issue_title: deprecate mapbox-upload in favor of mapbox-cli-py?
issue_body:
It'd be good to deprecate one of our CLI upload tools, [mapbox-upload](https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-upload), so we can focus development and fixes in a single repo. Mapbox-cli-py is our go-to CLI tool, and should be able to do all of the work mapbox-upload does.

On first glance, it doesn't do anything more than this CLI, so it shouldn't be a huge lift. I'll do more digging to make sure all features are carried over.

cc @rclark @willwhite @perrygeo @mattficke @sgillies

issue_title: change import to relative, so installation outside of site-packages works
issue_body:
I installed simplejson with PIP, then moved the package into my project's 'thirdparty' folder so my users don't need to install extra packages on their system once they download my code (these systems won't have PIP available, at least not reliably)

After changing my project code to import simplejson from my new location, I found this line had to be made a relative import, since my 'thirdparty' folder is not on the PATH:
https://github.com/simplejson/simplejson/blob/db2a216a858e8fd2cbfd53ac52c7972e5d3b3c5a/simplejson/encoder.py#L17

just changed to:
`from .decoder import PosInf`

I could also add my 'thirdparty' folder to my PYTHONPATH at run-time, but thought to avoid it. 
Please let me know if you think one way or the other is better.

issue_title: Suggestion: improve UX when editing new blog post
issue_body:
After creating a new empty blog post, it would be nice if there was a more direct way to edit the post content in Content Mode. 

If I click on Edit Post, an fill out some of the post content, it becomes directly editable in the Content Mode, and I can just click on it for editing. However when you create a new blog post, there is no content, and that option isn't directly editable alone. It would be nice if there was some default content that shows only under the following conditions.

1. The post is empty
2. We are in edit mode.

I sort of imagine creating a new, empty blog post, and then going directly to that page and clicking on some text like "This is the post content. Click here to edit me!"

```toml
[[source]]
url = "https://pypi.org/simple"
verify_ssl = true
name = "pypi"

[packages]
redis = "*"

[dev-packages]

[requires]
python_version = "3.6"
```
Note: This will only work if you get lines such as "Ignoring unknown cache-control directive" in the output from `pip`. I'm getting it on macOS.

issue_title: SessionSecurityMiddleware Client Activity Keep-Alive
issue_body:
I really like the all-in-one convenience of django-security, but the SessionSecurityMiddleware implementation lacks the client-side keep-alive available in [django-session-security](https://github.com/yourlabs/django-session-security).  The keep-alive is important to us because our product is used to guide a conversation with a customer so our users are often "active" on a page without server-side interaction.

Any interest adding a keep-alive feature to django-security?  If so, what approach would you prefer?  The licenses look compatible so it seems like any of the following would work:

 - Replace SessionSecurityMiddleware with the django-session-security implementation
 - Port the JS code to SessionSecurityMiddleware
 - Include both in django-security

issue_title: Better/Flexible logging support
issue_body:
Current logging support in streamparse could be more helpful when troubleshooting issues. While the logs are split into files for each bolt/spout, when things go wrong, its not clear as to where in the code a problem occurred. It would be better if the logger could emit the function name and the line number of the code along with the log message. 

However, this cannot be controlled by users as the formatter configuration is hardcoded like so (refer ipc.py):

formatter = logging.Formatter('%(asctime)s - %(name)s - %(levelname)s - %(message)s')

Worse, python logger does not allow us to fetch a logger's handler and set/modify its formatter. Thus, I would suggest that we change the logging format to this:

formatter = logging.Formatter("%(levelname)s %(asctime)s %(module)s:%(lineno)s %(funcName)s %(message)s")

If changing the code as above is not desirable, then the formatter string could become a configurable option. I believe that debugging issues will become significantly easier with this change.

issue_title: analytics.track() - asynchronous
issue_body:
Hi there,

I have been working with Segment's analytics-python module for a little while now.

Today I discovered that for example the `track` method is *asynchronous*.
I never realized this before, since I usually use it within services that are always up (listeners, queues, etc)

Today I had to do a simple script to read from a file and push some data into my Segment bucket. Surprisingly when I run the super simple script the data didn't get to my Segment debugger, although when I ran the same exact code in a python console it worked like a charm.

I finally saw the problem was that my script finished before the `analytics.track()` function was done.
I added an extra 10 seconds to wait for it to be executed and I made sure that was the problem indeed.

So, is there anyway to make the `track` method wait for itself? Any extra args I could put in? 
Callbacks? Any suggestions?

I took sometime to go through all the options I didn't figure out a way to do it,
any help is greatly appreciated.

Thanks for your time.

issue_title: [ENH] Min/Max for histograms computation
issue_body:
## Description
I was wondering if there is war to better define `exposure.histogram`.

I usually compute the histogram between 0. and 1. (for float images) but in scikit-image the histogram is computed between the min and max. I'm pretty sure some user apply the histogram without seeing that problem creating possibly bugs (the docstring is not really explicit on that subject).

I propose to add two optional argument to histogram `min` and `max` giving the possibility to the user to compute between the min/max value he wants. If they are put to None, the histogram is computed in the same way as we have now.

If you're not agree with that, maybe it could be interesting to add a comment about that on the docstring ?

I'm in Scipy-2016, so I can propose do it during the spring if you want ?

Code:
```
(pylint) sevagh:py-2.6-compat-linting $ cat example.py
#!/usr/bin/env python2

if __name__ == '__main__':
    mystr = 'Bad format string {} {} {}'.format('a',
                                                'b',
                                                'c')

    with open('/tmp/f1.txt', 'r') as a, open('/tmp/f2.txt', 'r') as b:
        print('Two withs in one line!')
(pylint) sevagh:py-2.6-compat-linting $
```

Pylintrc:
```
(pylint) sevagh:py-2.6-compat-linting $ cat .pylintrc
[MASTER]
load-plugins=saltpylint.minpyver,
    saltpylint.strings
minimum-python-version=2.6
[MESSAGES CONTROL]
disable=all

issue_title: WebLinkback: introduce limit on number of trackback registrations per IP, per second
issue_body:
Originally on 2013-01-10

Similarly to WebComment which can limit the number of comments submitted by a unique user in a given time window (CFG_WEBCOMMENT_TIMELIMIT_PROCESSING_COMMENTS_IN_SECONDS, CFG_WEBCOMMENT_TIMELIMIT_PROCESSING_REVIEWS_IN_SECONDS), it would be good to introduce a mechanism to limit the number of trackback registrations a given IP can submit in a chosen time window (eg. 1 per day?). 

The idea would be to limit a bit the possibilities for spammers to make use of the trackback feature (the implementation of the ticket might only have a very limited impact though. It could be checked if better alternatives exist, but on the other hand this solution should be trivial to implement).

```

Example for one INSPIRE worker node:

```
* 2012-03-28 13:08:30 -> IOError: failed to write data (webinterface_handler_wsgi.py:181:flush)
* 2012-03-28 13:10:50 -> IOError: failed to write data (webinterface_handler_wsgi.py:181:flush)

* 2012-03-28 13:50:03 -> IOError: failed to write data (webinterface_handler_wsgi.py:181:flush)
* 2012-03-28 13:51:49 -> IOError: failed to write data (webinterface_handler_wsgi.py:179:flush)
* 2012-03-28 13:53:57 -> IOError: failed to write data (webinterface_handler_wsgi.py:181:flush)
* 2012-03-28 13:56:10 -> IOError: failed to write data (webinterface_handler_wsgi.py:181:flush)
* 2012-03-28 13:58:20 -> IOError: failed to write data (webinterface_handler_wsgi.py:181:flush)
* 2012-03-28 13:59:36 -> IOError: failed to write data (webinterface_handler_wsgi.py:181:flush)
* 2012-03-28 13:59:37 -> IOError: failed to write data (webinterface_handler_wsgi.py:181:flush)
* 2012-03-28 14:02:51 -> IOError: failed to write data (webinterface_handler_wsgi.py:181:flush)
* 2012-03-28 14:03:03 -> IOError: failed to write data (webinterface_handler_wsgi.py:181:flush)
* 2012-03-28 14:27:21 -> IOError: failed to write data (webinterface_handler_wsgi.py:181:flush)
* 2012-03-28 14:29:57 -> IOError: failed to write data (webinterface_handler_wsgi.py:181:flush)
* 2012-03-28 14:30:02 -> IOError: failed to write data (webinterface_handler_wsgi.py:181:flush)
* 2012-03-28 14:30:11 -> IOError: failed to write data (webinterface_handler_wsgi.py:181:flush)
* 2012-03-28 14:34:22 -> IOError: failed to write data (webinterface_handler_wsgi.py:181:flush)
* 2012-03-28 14:52:29 -> IOError: failed to write data (webinterface_handler_wsgi.py:181:flush)
* 2012-03-28 14:54:26 -> IOError: failed to write data (webinterface_handler_wsgi.py:181:flush)
```

I guess these are never looked at by the admins and so probably mostly get ignored.  This means that logging them is not very useful. Already these exceptions were black-listed for email reporting.  In case of user storms, analysing the traceback and logging these possibly not exploitable exceptions just unnecessarily eats from available CPU time.

We can improve the situation by simply logging the exception title without doing traceback analysis.  That is, this will keep the exception count in the logs for stats and possible forensics and other purposes, but it will treat the exception body as not useful to keep, and so not logged.  This will make logging faster, log files smaller, and stuff.

There may be more exceptions of this sort where analysing and logging the exception body is not needed, so this feature may be generally useful.  New optional argument named like `perform_analysis=True` in `register_exception()` could do the job nicely.

P.S. Even better would be to try to fix WSGI connection write failures, of course.

issue_title: zope.exceptions.exceptionformatter and non-ASCII characters
issue_body:
In https://bugs.launchpad.net/zope.exceptions/+bug/98389, @mgedmin reported:

> I used `zope.exceptions.exceptionformatter.format_exception(as_html=False)` in an application to show tracebacks in views. I have encountered situations when `format_exception` returned me a list of UTF-8 strings (my system locale is UTF-8), other situations where I got a list of Unicode strings, and even situations where the list contained a mixture of UTF-8 and Unicode strings (causing UnicodeDecodeErrors in `''.join(...)`).

> The contract of the exception formatter should explicitly say whether it returns Unicode or not, and various formatting methods should take care to convert the strings as appropriate. I would prefer to get Unicode always.

> Source of 8-bit characters: `linecache.getline`, `traceback.format_exception_only`. Suspected source of Unicode strings: `__traceback_info__`/`__traceback_supplement__`.

> There are some calls to `str()` and, `log.Formatter` uses `cStringIO` -- these would break with Unicode strings.

> (Side issue: `TextExceptionFormatter` also sometimes performs HTML escaping.)

    527             ncol = np.median(ncol_raw)
--> 528             header_index = np.where(ncol_raw == ncol - 1)[0][-1]
    529             header = dataset[header_index]
    530             ds_header = dataset[header_index + 1:]

IndexError: index -1 is out of bounds for axis 0 with size 0
```
The format for those file is different

I rewrote `get_data_famafrench`, it's a bit simpler, it uses CSV and it works with all files from French's site (except 'BE-ME_Breakpoints' since it's header is invalid, quick fix).

Here's my (a bit dirty) code:
```python
def get_data_famafrench(name):
    def dateparse(x):
        x = x.strip()
        try: return pd.datetime.strptime(x, '%Y')
        except: pass
        try: return pd.datetime.strptime(x, '%Y%m')
        except: pass
        return pd.to_datetime(x)
        
    def try_periodicized(x):
        try: return x.to_period()
        except: pass
        return x

    # path of zip files
    zip_file_path = '{0}/{1}_CSV.zip'.format(_FAMAFRENCH_URL, name)

    with urlopen(zip_file_path) as url:
        raw = url.read()

    with tempfile.TemporaryFile() as tmpf:
        tmpf.write(raw)

        with ZipFile(tmpf, 'r') as zf:
            data = zf.open(zf.namelist()[0]).read().decode()

    endl = '\r\n'
    data = data.replace(3*endl, 2*endl)
    file_edges = np.array([edge.end() for edge in re.finditer(2*endl, data)])

    datasets = {}
    edges = zip(file_edges, file_edges[1:])
    for i, (left_edge, right_edge) in enumerate(edges):
        src = data[left_edge:right_edge]
        title = i
        
        if len(src) < 800:
            continue

        idx = src.find(',')
        if idx > 0:
            title = src[:idx].replace('\r\n', ' ').strip()
        src = src[idx:]

        df = pd.read_csv(StringIO('Date' + src), index_col=0,
                         parse_dates=[0], date_parser=dateparse)
                
        datasets[title] = try_periodicized(df)

    return datasets
```

To grab the full of downloadable data:
```python
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup

url = 'http://mba.tuck.dartmouth.edu/pages/faculty/ken.french/data_library.html'
root = BeautifulSoup(urlopen(url).read(), 'html.parser')

f = filter(lambda x: x[:4] == 'ftp/' and x[-8:] == '_CSV.zip',
           [l.attrs['href'] for l in root.findAll('a') if 'href' in l.attrs])

g = list(map(lambda x: x[4:-8], f))
```

Should I clean up everything, document a tad and submit a pull request ?

issue_title: Refactor portfolio level returns in analyze_sim_run notebook
issue_body:
Currently we compute portfolio level returns in the analyze_sim_run notebook using a weighted average of the returns to each algo in the portfolio.

It would be more proper to compute portfolio level returns from the aggregated portfolio state. Gus did take a stab at doing this, but we then discovered his first implementation had a bug in that it did not account for capital being rebalanced across algos monthly and erroneously treated that capital being added or removed as PNL.

The fix was to roll back to a weighted average of returns streams, but we'd prefer to have the aggregated portfolio state method properly handle capital in/out flows in the future. 

cc @gusgordon @twiecki

issue_title: Separable ChromaticObject with null SED at bandpass effective wavelength is undrawable.
issue_body:
Drawing separable `ChromaticObject`s currently works by first drawing the achromatic object at the effective wavelength of the filter in question, and then scaling this image by the wavelength integral of bandpass * SED.  This fails, however, if the value of the SED is zero at the effective wavelength of the filter, a situation that probably doesn't occur too often with realistic spectra/filters but can occur (and just did for my office-mate!) for contrived tophat filters and SEDs.

One possible solution would be to search for a fiducial wavelength (probably still starting at the filter effective wavelength, or maybe at the midpoint between the filter blue/red edges) for which the SED has non-zero flux.

def session(self):

            r = self._session.post(url, data=json.dumps(authentication_data))

issue_title: Skip Lambda Environment Supported Python Packages in Deployment Package
issue_body:
This is a feature request. 

I'm not sure if this functionality is always desired, but I noticed a difference while testing different combinations. The python 2.7 lambda environment already has boto3 available. So deploying a blank chalice app creates a package size of ~17K. However, when adding `boto3==1.3.1` to `requirements.txt` the size jumps up to 3.9MB. 

```
$ ls -lrth .chalice/deployments
-rw-r--r--  1 matt  staff    17K May 16 12:56 d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e-python2.7.zip
-rw-r--r--  1 matt  staff   3.9M May 16 12:56 781c6a4faff7afef80aeca0d4f1495c4-python2.7.zip
```

Based on this, maybe chalice could provide a flag to ignore lambda pre-built packages? This way packages can be specified in requirements.txt normally and when it duplicates what exists in the lambda environment, lambda size can be kept minimal. 

Obviously there are more details to think through. I just wanted to throw the idea out there.

issue_title: EVM: Symbolic execution on a path with multiplication on signed integer differs from concrete execution
issue_body:
### Manticore version
0e8c9b0bf2b7e9b8145254f86522867b67c8947f
Note: I am not using the last master, as I didn't update my script to change `m.SValue`

### Summary of the problem
There is a difference between the symbolic execution and the concrete execution when the execution has constraints on multiplication of signed integer.

### Step to reproduce the behavior
I am trying to test the following code:

```solidity
// bugMul.sol
contract Math{

    function mul(int a, int b) external returns(int){
        int c = a * b;  
        if(a==0) return 0;
        if(b==0) return 0;
        require(c / a == b);
        return c;
    }
}
```

I want to check that all the states that are reverted are due to an overflow (== if a state reverted it's because of an overflow).
My script is:
```python
from manticore.ethereum import ManticoreEVM
from manticore.core.smtlib import Operators, solver


m = ManticoreEVM()

owner_account = m.create_account(balance=1000)

with open('bugMul.sol') as f:
    contract = m.solidity_create_contract(f, owner=owner_account)


contract.mul(m.SValue, m.SValue)

# from https://github.com/trailofbits/manticore/blob/master/manticore/ethereum.py
def signed_mul_overflow(a, b):
    '''
    Sign extend the value to 512 bits and check the result can be represented
     in 256. Following there is a 32 bit excerpt of this condition:
    a  *  b           +00000000000000000 +00000000000000001 +0000000003fffffff +0000000007fffffff +00000000080000001 +000000000bfffffff +000000000ffffffff
    +0000000000000000  +0000000000000000  +0000000000000000  +0000000000000000  +0000000000000000  +0000000000000000  +0000000000000000  +0000000000000000
    +0000000000000001  +0000000000000000  +0000000000000001  +000000003fffffff  +000000007fffffff  +0000000080000001  +00000000bfffffff  +00000000ffffffff
    +000000003fffffff  +0000000000000000  +000000003fffffff *+0fffffff80000001 *+1fffffff40000001 *+1fffffffbfffffff *+2fffffff00000001 *+3ffffffec0000001
    +000000007fffffff  +0000000000000000  +000000007fffffff *+1fffffff40000001 *+3fffffff00000001 *+3fffffffffffffff *+5ffffffec0000001 *+7ffffffe80000001
    +0000000080000001  +0000000000000000  +0000000080000001 *+1fffffffbfffffff *+3fffffffffffffff *+4000000100000001 *+600000003fffffff *+800000007fffffff
    +00000000bfffffff  +0000000000000000  +00000000bfffffff *+2fffffff00000001 *+5ffffffec0000001 *+600000003fffffff *+8ffffffe80000001 *+bffffffe40000001
    +00000000ffffffff  +0000000000000000  +00000000ffffffff *+3ffffffec0000001 *+7ffffffe80000001 *+800000007fffffff *+bffffffe40000001 *+fffffffe00000001
    '''
    mul = Operators.SEXTEND(a, 256, 512) * Operators.SEXTEND(b, 256, 512)
    cond = Operators.OR(mul < -(1 << 255), mul >= (1 << 255))
    return cond

print "run checks"

# Check that all the reverted state are due to an overflow
for state in m.terminated_states:
    # Retrieve inputs
    data_mul = state.platform.transactions[1].data
    x = Operators.CONCAT(256, *data_mul[4:36])
    y = Operators.CONCAT(256, *data_mul[36:68])
    input_mul = x * y

    # check for overflow
    with state as tmp_state:

        # Add the constraint that there is no overflow, if it's SAT, a bug is found
        tmp_state.constrain(Operators.NOT(signed_mul_overflow(x, y)))

        if solver.check(tmp_state.constraints):
            # Get the values, one by one
            x_solved = solver.get_value(tmp_state.constraints, x)
            tmp_state.constrain(x == x_solved)

            y_solved = solver.get_value(tmp_state.constraints, y)
            tmp_state.constrain(y == y_solved)

            # As the path reverted, z is computed from the input
            z_solved = solver.get_value(tmp_state.constraints, input_mul)

            print "Correct mul found in reverted states!, %x %x %x"%(x_solved, y_solved, z_solved)

            # Generate a testcase file 
            m.generate_testcase(tmp_state, 'BugMul')
            print "See "+m.workspace
```

It found a reverted path for which there is no overflow. For example:
```
Function call:
mul(1393966716111906825181365749768185729516032L,20766652442136795645389052729894022L) -> REVERT (*)
```

When I execute the same contract with the concrete input provided by Manticore, I obtain a different path (which does not revert):
```python
from manticore.ethereum import ManticoreEVM

m = ManticoreEVM()

owner_account = m.create_account(balance=1000)

with open('bugMul.sol') as f:
    contract = m.solidity_create_contract(f, owner=owner_account)


contract.mul(0x100080000100000000000000000000000200, 0x3ffe000ffc3360000000e00702486)

print m.workspace

m.finalize()
```
Gives:
```
Function call:
mul(1393966716111906825181365749768185729516032L,20766652442136795645389052729894022L) -> RETURN 
return: 28948022309402739193010462882933984814686440406855823881066961041124709960704L 
```

I tested these values on Remix, the concrete execution is correct. 

My supposition is that the path predicate generated with multiplication on signed integer is incorrect, and leads to an input valuation that does not match its concrete execution.


### Expected behavior
The symbolic execution should have the same outcome than the concrete execution.

### Actual behavior
The symbolic execution generates input that leads to another path.

issue_title: Populate with Python 3.6: "TypeError: expected bytes, str found"
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm trying to load parameters of a model using populate:

```python
w.populate(load_from, '/w')
```

where `w` is a parameter and `load_from` is the file path string.

I get the following exception:

```
  File "_dynet.pyx", line 563, in _dynet.Parameters.populate
  File "_dynet.pyx", line 575, in _dynet.Parameters.populate_from_textfile
  File "stringsource", line 15, in string.from_py.__pyx_convert_string_from_py_std__in_string
TypeError: expected bytes, str found
```

I saved the parameters using the following code:

```python
w.save(save_to, '/w')
```

where `save_to` is the same string as `load_from`.

I work with python 3.6 and I suspect that the exception stems from the difference between strings in python 2 and 3: http://docs.cython.org/en/latest/src/tutorial/strings.html.

Thanks!

issue_title: Picking and setting values of tensors at particular batch
issue_body:
Hi,

It would be nice that `pick` could support retrieving values from a specific batch. Similarly, we could have a `set` operation to allow setting values of particular rows/columns/batches. This is extremely useful to allow for mixed batching/unbatching operations. E.g.,

```
1. Batched LSTM encoding of inputs
2. Use `pick` to get encoding results for each batch. Build operations for each batch dynamically.
3. Use `set` to merge data from different batches and continue with batched operation for efficiency.
```

issue_title: CyclicalSGDTrainer: learning_rate never updated with default Trainer constructor
issue_body:
Hi,
I don't know if this is a bug, but it really is counter-intuitive!

In the Cyclical SGD trainer contructor, gamma is set to zero by default which cancels any learning_rate update
See function cyclic_update_eta, l.249 in current master.
The update is multiplied by itth power of gamma, which is zero by default. See:

https://github.com/clab/dynet/blob/b07411ca75e74afe3f19f6fbda5646c5ddd6f4b2/dynet/training.h#L249

issue_title: `memote report snapshot` raises UnicodeDecodeError on localized Windows 10
issue_body:
When running `memote report snapshot` on the default model on my Windows 10 system using memote 0.5.1, I get a UnicodeDecodeError from encodings/cp1252.py when memote tries to open the html template for the report. Thus, the HTML report never gets written.

From what I can google, the problem is that Python 3 will sometimes [assume the filesystem's default encoding](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/42070668/python-3-default-encoding-cp1252).

The contributing guidelines requests patches to come with tests, but unfortunately I was unable to simulate this error on Linux (CentOS) using `locale.setlocale()`. Nevertheless, I propose a patch below for your consideration.

Steps to reproduce:
Run `memote new` accepting all default suggestions, then `memote report snapshot`. Or use the "more easily debuggable code" below.

<details>
<summary>More easily debuggable code to reproduce the issue</summary>

```python
"""Easily debuggable call to memote, similar to "memote report snapshot"."""

from memote.suite import api
from memote.suite.cli import callbacks


# Adapted from memote/suite/cli/runner.py
model = "model.xml"
filename = "test_memote.html"
pytest_args = []
solver = "glpk"
custom = None, None

model = callbacks.validate_model(None, None, model)

# Adapted from memote/suite/reporting/reports/snapshot.py
model.solver = solver
_, results = api.test_model(model, results=True, pytest_args=pytest_args,
                            custom=custom)
api.snapshot_report(results, filename)
```
</details>

<details>
<summary>Traceback</summary>

```bash
[...snip lots of regular test output...]
======== 37 failed, 40 passed, 13 skipped, 2 warnings in 5.48 seconds =========
>>> api.snapshot_report(results, filename)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "C:\git\memote\memote\suite\api.py", line 135, in snapshot_report
    file_h.write(report.render_html())
  File "C:\git\memote\memote\suite\reporting\reports\snapshot.py", line 55, in render_html
    template = Template(file_handle.read())
  File "C:\Anaconda3\lib\encodings\cp1252.py", line 23, in decode
    return codecs.charmap_decode(input,self.errors,decoding_table)[0]
UnicodeDecodeError: 'charmap' codec can't decode byte 0x8d in position 391134: character maps to <undefined>
```
</details>

<details>
<summary>System Information</summary>

```
Windows 10 version 1709
Python 3.6.3

```

issue_title: Storing an Entity as an indexed property value always fails
issue_body:
From the [spec][1]:

> If a value is itself an entity, it cannot have `indexed` set to `true`.

This means when the `entity_value` is [added][2] we should set `indexed` to `False` on the property. (This is currently not easy, see #403, #404 and #512.)

Or maybe we should just document that users need to add a property name to `exclude_from_indexes` if that property is an `Entity`.

-----

To replicate:

```python
>>> import os
>>> from gcloud import datastore
>>> from gcloud.datastore.entity import Entity
>>> from gcloud.datastore.key import Key
>>> 
>>> datastore.set_default_connection()
>>> dataset_id = os.getenv('GCLOUD_TESTS_DATASET_ID')
>>> datastore.set_default_dataset_id(dataset_id)
>>> 
>>> e = Entity(key=Key('Foo', 1))
>>> e2 = Entity(key=Key('Foo', 2))
>>> e['bar'] = e2
>>> e.save()
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "gcloud/datastore/entity.py", line 144, in save
    exclude_from_indexes=self.exclude_from_indexes)
  File "gcloud/datastore/connection.py", line 495, in save_entity
    result = self.commit(dataset_id, mutation)
  File "gcloud/datastore/connection.py", line 376, in commit
    datastore_pb.CommitResponse)
  File "gcloud/datastore/connection.py", line 100, in _rpc
    data=request_pb.SerializeToString())
  File "gcloud/datastore/connection.py", line 77, in _request
    raise six.moves.http_client.HTTPException(message)
httplib.HTTPException: Request failed with status code 400. Error was: Entity value is indexed.
```

and notice it doesn't fail if the property is correctly unindexed:

```python
>>> e = Entity(key=Key('Foo', 1), exclude_from_indexes=('bar',))
>>> e['bar'] = e2
>>> e.save()
>>> e.key.delete()
```

[1]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-datastore/blob/21e029b2657ebb15fef5429c90c4315326855fd1/proto/datastore_v1.proto#L142-L144
[2]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/blob/0525530ad7a76e408ca2e842b883d49c353ed56d/gcloud/datastore/helpers.py#L245

issue_title: Content# counts in Kolibri are inaccurate
issue_body:
## Summary

We appear to be counting `File` objects rather than `ContentNode` objects in returning content/resource counts on the content import page. This is different (and less useful) from what is reported on the Studio side.

The correct code for counting would be something like:
`ContentNode.objects.get(id=root_node_id).get_descendants().exclude(kind=content_kinds.TOPIC).count()`

## System information

 - Version: 0.5.0

## How to reproduce

1. Download content
1. Go to content page and look at count
1. Cry

## Screenshots

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/618416/30395216-4a3cd49e-987a-11e7-872b-0ff78654aeb3.png)

## Real-life consequences (anything community should be aware of, for instance how it affects your deployment)

Users have reported in confusion on the mismatches between the Studio counts and the counts in Kolibri.
